hook: You've spent this course mastering derivatives — finding how things change — but what about working backwards to recover the original quantity from a rate? Whether it's computing the total distance from a velocity function or finding the exact area under a curve, derivatives alone can't get you there. This unit hands you the other half of calculus, completing a beautiful relationship that will let you solve problems neither algebra nor differentiation could touch on their own.
concept: Integration is the process of accumulating infinitely many infinitesimal pieces into a whole — the unifying idea being that summation and differentiation are, remarkably, inverse operations, formalized as the 'Fundamental Theorem of Calculus'.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to reverse the differentiation process, finding families of functions $F(x) + C$ whose derivative returns a given function $f(x)$.
Build the rigorous definition of the definite integral $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ by approximating areas with increasingly fine rectangular slices called Riemann sums.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 1 and 2)
topic_roadmap.description: Master the theorem that unites differentiation and integration, using it to evaluate definite integrals exactly and to differentiate accumulation functions in one step.
Apply the substitution rule to integrate composite functions by introducing a new variable $u$, effectively reversing the chain rule.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Integrals of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
topic_roadmap.description: Extend your integration toolkit to evaluate integrals involving $e^x$, $a^x$, and $\ln x$, including cases where substitution reveals a hidden logarithm.
Integrate all six trigonometric functions by combining known derivatives, strategic identities, and substitution to handle both basic and composite trig expressions.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Numerical Integration: Trapezoidal and Simpson's Rules (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Approximate definite integrals for functions without elementary antiderivatives using the Trapezoidal and Simpson's Rules, and analyze the error in each estimate.
